summary: My name is Masayo Matsumoto, born in 1893 in Nagano Prefecture as the second daughter of Matasaku and Toshi Matsumoto. I was raised in a family with a strong Shinto background and experienced many hardships, including illness and family struggles. Despite these challenges, I pursued education, became a teacher, and later embraced Christianity, which deeply transformed my life. My father named me Masayo, meaning 'to give politics,' reflecting his interest in politics and hopes for me.

I was born and raised in Nagano Prefecture, where my family lived and where my father served as a village chief. It was there that I experienced my childhood, including the strict Shinto practices and my early education. Nagano was also the place where I first encountered Christianity and began attending church services despite the distance. My father's involvement in local politics and community projects, like building a bridge over the Kiso River, were significant parts of our life in Nagano.

I was born in 1893, which was the 26th year of the Meiji era, a time of great change in Japan. My father lived through the Meiji period, witnessing the introduction of trains and modernization efforts. The Meiji emperor's influence was present in my schooling, where I was honored to guard pictures of the emperor and empress. This era shaped much of my family's history and the societal expectations we lived under.

Our family was given the surname 'Minamoto' by our local lord, connecting us to the Minamoto clan. This affiliation was part of our heritage and status in the community. My father, who was part of this lineage, attended school carrying two swords, reflecting the clan's samurai background. The Minamoto name carried weight and respect in our village and shaped our identity.

My father went down to Tokyo to study despite his father's opposition, marking a pivotal moment in his life. Tokyo represented opportunity and modern education during that time, especially as trains had just been invented. It was in Tokyo that my father struggled as a working student and met the rickshaw driver whose daughter he later married. His experiences in Tokyo influenced our family's future greatly.

The Obon festival was a memorable event in my family's story, especially because my father's future wife sang a beautiful song during it. This festival was part of our village traditions and held cultural significance. It was during Obon that my father first noticed the woman he would marry, despite family opposition due to her tenant farmer background.

When I became very sick during my teacher's college years, my father sold property to send me to Nagoya University Hospital for six months. There, I was treated for rheumatism, pleurisy, heart dilation, and nervous debilitation. This hospital stay was crucial for my recovery and allowed me to return to school as a returning student, which was rare at the time.

I graduated from teacher's college in the fourth year of the Taishō period, a time when Japan was experiencing social and political changes. This period framed my early adult life and teaching career. Despite my health challenges, I completed my education during this era, which influenced my perspectives and opportunities.

Shinto was my family's traditional religion, and my father was part of the Shinto priesthood for years. Our home had a Shinto altar where my mother made us pray daily, and strict rules governed our behavior around it. However, my father struggled with belief in Shinto gods, seeing them as human creations, which caused tension in our family. I eventually found Shinto practices too strict and sought spiritual comfort elsewhere.

At one point, my father became interested in the Tenri sect, reflecting his search for spiritual meaning beyond Shintoism. This interest came during a difficult time when he considered moving to Nagoya with me. Tenrikyo represented an alternative faith path that my father explored as he struggled with traditional beliefs.

As a child, I prayed at Suwa Shrine, especially when I stopped eating my favorite food to wish for my mother's recovery. The shrine was a significant spiritual place near Mt. Ontake, where my mother taught us to bow whenever we passed. Suwa Shrine was part of our Shinto tradition and family rituals during my upbringing.

Reading the Bible was a transformative experience for me after I encountered Christianity. The congregation gave me a Bible, and I was moved by its teachings, which were very different from Shinto beliefs. Studying the Bible helped me find spiritual security and motivated me to pursue baptism despite family opposition.

I read verses from the Gospel of Matthew, chapter 34, which initially confused me but deepened my engagement with Christian teachings. This scripture was part of my early Bible study as I explored my new faith. It symbolized my journey from traditional beliefs to Christianity.

Reverend Kanzo Ogawa was the assistant minister at the church I attended, and he was a follower of Reverend Kagawa. Meeting him and the congregation inspired me to learn more about Christianity. He and his mother were kind and supportive, helping me feel welcomed despite my background.

Reverend Kanzo Ogawa was one of Reverend Kagawa Shūsui's followers, which connected me indirectly to Kagawa's influence in the Christian community. Though I did not meet Kagawa personally, his followers shaped my spiritual path. Kagawa's reputation added credibility to the church where I found faith.

The assistant minister's mother was related to the Byakkotai, an aristocratic group, and she was known for her grace and kindness. She was the daughter of a higher Byakkotai member and connected to the Tokugawa family. Her background impressed me and showed the diverse social ties within the Christian community.

The assistant minister's mother was related to the Tokugawa clan through her aristocratic lineage connected to the Byakkotai. This connection highlighted the mingling of old samurai families with new religious movements like Christianity. It was fascinating to see such historical ties within the church community I joined.

Reverend Hiraiwa of the Methodist Church came to Nagano Prefecture as a superintendent and deeply moved me with his sermons. His words encouraged me to pursue Christianity despite my parents' opposition, assuring me that baptism would ultimately bring happiness to my family. The Methodist Church played a key role in my spiritual awakening and decision to become a Christian.

Nagano Prefecture was my birthplace and the setting for much of my early life, including my father's role as village chief and my initial exposure to Shinto and Christianity. It was also where Reverend Hiraiwa preached and where I attended church despite the distance. Nagano shaped my cultural and spiritual identity deeply.

At one point, I was living in Gifu Prefecture while attending church meetings in the evenings. The journey home was often dark and lonely, but singing hymns gave me courage against fears like foxes on the mountain trail. Gifu was part of my Christian journey and personal growth during those years.

My father, as village chief, helped build a bridge across the Kiso River with a neighboring village chief. This project was important for our community's development and showed my father's dedication to public service. Crossing that bridge was part of my daily life and symbolized the connection between villages.

Christianity was a faith I discovered after reading about it and attending hymn lessons, which contrasted sharply with my family's Shinto beliefs. I was baptized despite opposition from my father and others, finding in Christianity a spiritual security and community kindness I had not known before. My faith influenced my life decisions and relationships profoundly.

I was baptized by Reverend Kunihara from Nagoya Central Church, marking a joyful and transformative moment in my life. His role as minister was significant in my acceptance into the Christian faith. Kunihara's support helped me overcome family resistance and embrace my new beliefs fully.

I saw terrible things among ministers in Los Angeles, which influenced my decision not to encourage my sister to become a Christian. Although my sister married into a Shinto family and was unhappy, I felt conflicted about sharing my faith with her because of what I witnessed abroad. Los Angeles represented a cautionary example in my spiritual journey.

Zen Buddhism was part of the religious landscape I grew up around, but it did not play a direct role in my personal faith journey. My family's religion was Shinto, and later I embraced Christianity, which contrasted with Zen Buddhist practices. Though I was aware of Zen Buddhism, it was not central to my story.

The President of the United States was not directly mentioned in my story, but my life eventually connected to the U.S. through my travels and experiences abroad. My family's hopes for me to earn money overseas reflect the broader ties between Japan and America during my lifetime. The U.S. represented opportunity and a new world beyond my Japanese upbringing.

During my schooling, I was honored to guard pictures of the Emperor and Empress of Japan, a duty I took seriously despite my frequent absences due to illness. This responsibility made me feel connected to my country and its traditions. The Emperor's presence was a symbol of national pride and cultural identity in my life.

entity: Coronation of the Emperor of Japan
summary: The coronation of the Emperor of Japan was not explicitly mentioned in my story, but the reverence for the Emperor was evident in my school duties and community life. Protecting the Emperor's pictures was part of the respect shown during such national events. These traditions shaped the environment in which I grew up.

Manchuria was not directly referenced in my personal narrative, so I do not have specific experiences or memories related to it. My story focused more on my life in Japan and my Christian faith journey. Thus, Manchuria remains outside the scope of my recollections.

Aoyama University was not mentioned in my story, so I have no direct connection or experiences related to it. My education was at a teacher's college and involved Nagoya University Hospital for my treatment. Therefore, Aoyama University does not figure in my personal history.

Yamanashi Prefecture was not part of my narrative or experiences, so I have no memories or stories connected to it. My life centered mainly around Nagano and Gifu Prefectures. Thus, Yamanashi remains outside my personal history.

California was indirectly referenced through my observations of ministers in Los Angeles, which is located there. My experiences in California influenced my views on Christianity and family decisions, especially regarding my sister's faith. California represented a place where I saw both opportunity and challenges within the Christian community.

Azabu Junior High School was not mentioned in my story, so I have no personal experiences or connections to it. My education was focused on teacher's college and earlier schooling in Nagano Prefecture. Therefore, Azabu Junior High School is not part of my narrative.
